title: Dog One Pupil Larger Than the Other: Causes and Urgent Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog has one pupil larger than the other (anisocoria), it may be a medical emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary attention is required.
content_html:
TL;DR: Anisocoria is a medical emergency where a dog's pupils are different sizes, signaling potential neurological damage or severe eye injury that requires immediate veterinary attention.

What is anisocoria and why are my dog's pupils different sizes?

Anisocoria is the medical term for when a dog’s pupils are two different sizes. One eye may be constricted (small), while the other is dilated (large). This is not a disease itself, but rather a clinical sign that something is affecting the eye's nerves or the brain's ability to control the iris muscles.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my dog's pupils are unequal?

Yes, anisocoria is considered a high-priority veterinary emergency. Because the eyes are closely linked to the central nervous system, unequal pupils can indicate serious conditions such as:

  • Head trauma
  • Glaucoma
  • Corneal injuries
  • Internal inflammation

Sudden changes in pupil size can lead to permanent blindness or indicate a life-threatening neurological issue if left untreated.

How does taking a photo of my dog's eyes help the veterinarian with triage?

If you are calling an emergency clinic or using a telehealth service, taking a clear photo of your dog's eyes can be vital. A photo captures the specific state of the eyes for the medical team by:

  • Recording the exact degree of the pupil size difference at a specific moment.
  • Helping veterinarians determine the immediate severity of the situation.
  • Allowing the vet to see accompanying signs such as redness, cloudiness, or discharge.
  • Helping the emergency staff prioritize your dog's care upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Anisocoria, or one pupil being larger than the other, can be a sign of several ophthalmic and neurological emergencies in dogs. Ophthalmic causes include acute glaucoma, indicated by a dilated, fixed, or sluggish pupil, bulbar conjunctival venous congestion, corneal edema, and a firm globe. Neurological causes include intracranial disease affecting the optic chiasm, optic tracts, or occipital cortex, such as from meningiomas or lymphosarcoma; and retrobulbar visual tract issues such as from hydrocephalus or central nervous system neoplasia. Anisocoria can also be associated with sudden acute retinal degeneration syndrome or optic neuritis. In cases of acute blindness with normal pupillary light reflexes, consider central blindness due to cardiac arrest during anesthesia, seizures, or severe head trauma.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: Can I wait until the morning to see a vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because eye and neurological conditions can deteriorate rapidly, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if you notice unequal pupil sizes.

faqs.question: Are there other signs I should look for?
faqs.answer: Yes. Look for squinting, pawing at the eye, a change in eye color, lethargy, or loss of balance, as these often accompany anisocoria.

faqs.question: Could it just be a scratch on the eye?
faqs.answer: While a corneal ulcer or scratch can cause pupil changes due to pain, only a veterinarian can distinguish between a simple scratch and a more severe internal or neurological issue.
